news_summary: Today's news highlights the positive impact of ancient natural remedies, the importance of transparency in scientific progress, and a creative move to celebrate wildlife through currency design.
news_summary_fr: L'actualité d'aujourd'hui met en avant l'impact positif des remèdes naturels anciens, l'importance de la transparence dans les progrès scientifiques et une initiative créative visant à célébrer la faune sauvage à travers la conception de la monnaie.
news_summary_es: Las noticias de hoy destacan el impacto positivo de los remedios naturales ancestrales, la importancia de la transparencia en el progreso científico y una iniciativa creativa para celebrar la vida silvestre a través del diseño de la moneda.

articles.title: How our ancestors used mushrooms to change the course of human history
articles.summary: Mushrooms have been used by ancient humans for millennia, but archaeologists have only just uncovered their pivotal role in shaping civilisation
articles.content:
Mushrooms have been used by ancient humans for millennia, but archaeologists have only just uncovered their pivotal role in shaping civilisation

Alex Hyde/naturepl.com

Seventy-five years ago, in the depths of a peat bog in Yorkshire, UK, archaeologists made a startling discovery: the perfectly preserved remains of dozens of 11,000-year-old mushrooms. Carefully cut and intentionally scorched, it is thought that these polypores were used by nomadic Mesolithic hunter-gatherers as tinder on their travels, acting as the earliest known portable fire kit.

The discovery was so unlikely because of the near-impossibility of mushrooms being preserved long enough for archaeologists to analyse them. Fungi are composed mostly of water, meaning they rapidly rot and disappear, and so are almost totally absent from the fossil record, says Hannah O’Regan at the University of Nottingham in the UK. As such, the unexpected ways that mushrooms helped our ancient ancestors survive have long remained secret, seemingly lost to time. Decades passed before we learned more.

But in the past few years, new tools have finally allowed us to identify fungal DNA and micro-residues in the mouths, utensils and clothing of prehistoric humans. These breakthroughs are highlighting how a hidden fungi kingdom fed, healed and warmed our Stone Age ancestors – with recent discoveries even illustrating how fungi provided social glue that helped hold early farming communities together, paving the way for the civilisation we live in today.

“By revealing the invisible evidence in the archaeological record, we now see how fungal connections helped the earliest Neolithic communities to develop an understanding of their land and their society,” says Li Lui at Stanford University in California. “These methods had never been applied to archaeology before.” The diversity of mushrooms being unearthed, says Laura Weyrich at Pennsylvania State University, is “helping to change our perspective on past humans”.

The first fungi foragers

Until about a decade ago, the conversation around prehistoric diets was limited to meat and plants, with mushrooms very much off the menu due to their archaeological obscurity. “Neanderthals, for example, have been labelled as carnivorous as polar bears,” says Weyrich. Yet in 2017, her team’s analysis of DNA in Palaeolithic dental plaque revealed that a group of Neanderthals in what is now Belgium supplemented their meaty meals with gray shag mushrooms, while those from a cave in northern Spain dined on split gill mushrooms.

Free newsletter Sign up to The Weekly The best of New Scientist, including long-reads, culture, podcasts and news, each week. Sign up to newsletter

One member of the Spanish group, who lived around 48,000 years ago, even chewed on grasses that had been colonised by penicillin moulds, and may have deliberately sought out this antibiotic fungus as medication for a dental abscess. “That particular individual would have been in an enormous amount of pain, and is the only person that we detected [penicillin] in,” says Weyrich. “That causes us to question, did they actually know that there was some sort of medicinal benefit by consuming it?”

It is impossible to say with certainty whether Neanderthals intentionally self-medicated with fungi, but the discovery that two separate communities ate mushrooms transformed our understanding of this lost human species.

Stable isotope analysis, which has been used since the late 1970s to detect dietary markers in ancient skeletons, was previously thought to show that Neanderthals were hypercarnivores due to the extremely high nitrogen values in their bones. “But what can also raise some of those nitrogen values is the contribution of mushrooms,” says Weyrich. “And in the environments that Neanderthals were living in, it’s much more likely that they were eating a diversity of animals, plants and mushrooms.”

Until recently, it had been impossible to detect the presence of fungi in ancient diets because their isotopic values had never been analysed or catalogued, creating an archaeological blind spot. “Isotope data has always been interpreted as, ‘this is meat, this is plants, and that’s what they ate’,” says Justina Stonytė, whose ongoing research at Lithuania’s Center for Physical Sciences and Technology is helping to fill this knowledge gap. By measuring the isotopic signatures of mushrooms for the first time, her work is revealing that their carbon and nitrogen values overlap with those of both plants and meat, meaning fungi may have been hiding in archaeological dietary signals all along.

“ We called our paper ‘The missing mushrooms’. Because they must be there, we just can’t find them “

Unfortunately, this broad overlap makes it difficult to identify individual mushroom species from isotope data alone, although some fungi do stand out as prime suspects. “We have found that Boletus-type mushrooms are quite close to meat in their nitrogen values, and we can expect ancient humans to have eaten them,” says Stonytė. Boletus mushrooms, better known as porcini, are among the most popular edible fungi in the world and, crucially, spores from this group have been found in the dental plaque of Palaeolithic humans from around 15,000 years ago in Spain.

Shortly after that discovery in 2015, O’Regan and her colleagues authored a paper suggesting that isotopic signals previously attributed to meat consumption could, in fact, reflect the foraging of Boletus or other mushrooms. “We called our paper ‘The missing mushrooms’,” she says. “Because they must be there, we just can’t find them.”

Mushroom fires

Those Boletus spores remain the only preserved remains – excluding DNA – that prove consumption in the deep past, and we are yet to find any preserved edible fungi associated with human remains. Instead, the fire fungi discovered in Yorkshire offer some of the most straightforward evidence for prehistoric mushroom use.

The Mesolithic site of Star Carr, where those peat-coated tinder fungi were discovered back in 1950, yielded a total of 82 ancient mushrooms, all wonderfully preserved by their anaerobic, waterlogged surroundings. Of those, 76 have been identified as Fomes fomentarius, also known as the tinder fungus due to its flammability when processed into a felt-like material called amadou.

An archaeologist at the Mesolithic site of Star Carr in Yorkshire, UK United National Photographers/Shuttersto​ck

The fungi had been both harvested and processed, says Harry Robson at York University, UK, who took part in excavations at Star Carr between 2008 and 2015. “Some had very clear cut marks on the exterior, with bits removed in order to access the inner body of the fungus itself,” he says. In total, 54 of the fungi showed signs of having been scraped and cut into amadou, while 41 were charred.

Researchers believe these bracket mushrooms were vital to the survival of migrating hunter-gatherers, enabling them to stay warm and cook food while on the move. At Star Carr, they were found alongside rolls of paper-thin birch bark, and Robson says they were probably used in conjunction with this material to create a lightweight, compact and highly reliable fire-starting kit that could be used to create a fire without breaking stride. “If you’ve got the equipment and apparatus to transport fire, then that is a game changer for nomadic societies,” he says.

Further examples of tinder fungi have emerged from the boggy ground at the 7300-year-old site of La Draga in Spain, as well as at Mullerup in Denmark, Hohen Viecheln in Germany and Vis I in Russia. The presence of amadou at such a variety of archaeological sites suggests that this portable pyro-technology was in use 11,000 years ago and continued to play a role throughout the Neolithic period, attesting to its unique value to prehistoric humans.

Yet it is the famous mummified Ötzi the Iceman – who lived some 5300 years ago in the Italian Alps – who provides the celebrity endorsement for F. fomentarius. Within a leather pouch preserved alongside Ötzi, researchers found amadou created from this mushroom, alongside his trusty pyrite strike-a-light. Pyrite fragments were even found lodged between the spongy amadou fibres, leaving no doubt that the iceman’s fire fungi had been ignited on his solo travels.

Ötzi’s mushrooms

Amadou wasn’t the only fungal innovation Ötzi carried with him on his final journey. Threaded on leather thongs that probably hung from his belt, archaeologists found two dried birch polypore mushrooms.

The researchers who discovered these fungi back in 1998 interpreted their use as medicinal, noting that Ötzi may have ingested them to treat a whipworm infestation in his intestines. Subsequent analyses have highlighted the anti-parasitic, antimicrobial, anti-cancer and immune-boosting properties of birch polypore.

Yet when the contents of Ötzi’s stomach were examined, there was no indication that he had ingested birch polypore mushrooms. This result weakened the medical hypothesis, and it is only now that research is finally providing an alternative explanation.

“ He was such a skilled hunter and survivalist, but fishing has always been overlooked when it comes to Ötzi “

Mariana Villani is currently investigating the traditional uses of the various mushrooms that Ötzi encountered as part of her research at Cardiff University and Royal Botanic Gardens, Kew, in the UK. Noting that Otzi carried the birch polypore mushrooms on his hunting belt, she has a radically new and as-yet unpublished idea regarding their function: as fishing floats.

Villani draws parallels between Ötzi’s fungi and others mentioned in an 18th-century Swedish ethnography, which describes their use as buoyancy aids for fishing nets. Both mushrooms have a beaded shape, mirroring the design of traditional cork fishing floats and the modern plastic variety. She points out that Ötzi also carried pieces of cordage and net, as well as a bone awl that, she says, “really looks like a fishing hook”. Villani has even conducted her own experiments using dried birch polypore mushrooms as fishing floats, both in Ötzi’s native Alpine valley and in her home country of Brazil.

“I caught eight fish in 3 hours!” she says. Villani found that, when coated in beeswax, the mushroom floats remained buoyant for several hours, and could be re-dried and reused multiple times with no loss of function. “The way the mushrooms were just hanging on Ötzi’s belt would have been perfect for drying them after fishing,” she says.

Unfortunately, however, no fish were found in Ötzi’s stomach, indicating that his final meal didn’t include any aquatic prey. There is also a distinct lack of direct evidence for Neolithic fishing practices in his native Schnalstal valley, although nets, fish traps, harpoons and canoes have been found at Stone Age sites along the shores of several nearby Alpine lakes.

Villani, though, is convinced. “He was such a skilled hunter and survivalist, but fishing has always been overlooked when it comes to Ötzi. If you look at these mushrooms, the most likely explanation for me is that they were used as fishing floats,” she says.

Fish may not have been the only food that Ötzi accessed with the help of fungi. Honey was probably a “staple food” for his community, according to Villani, and numerous ethnographic reports from recent times describe hunter-gatherers burning F. fomentarius to smoke beehives in order to harvest this resource. Amazingly, certain compounds in these tinder fungi protect honeybees from the notorious Varroa destructor mite, which is currently decimating colonies worldwide.

Despite a lack of solid evidence linking Ötzi to this practice, Villani’s research builds a case for it by highlighting the abundance of bee-friendly fungi in the Schnalstal valley. Puffballs, for instance, are among the most prominent local mushrooms, and Indigenous communities worldwide burn these spore-filled spheres – often in conjunction with F. fomentarius – to anaesthetise bees while harvesting honey.

“Puffballs are one of the most useful mushrooms ever, so it’s very likely that they were used by Ötzi and his contemporaries,” says Villani.

Tinder fungus was used to spark fires thousands of years ago Nature Picture Library/Alamy

Fungal fermentation

Pastoral communities like Ötzi’s began to appear when the last glacial period gave way to the present Holocene Epoch some 11,700 years ago, with the changing climate allowing humans to transition from hunter-gatherers to sedentary agriculturalists. Numerous factors contributed to this seismic shift. The emergence of new fungal allies, though, is one that is largely underappreciated.

Discoveries made by Liu and her colleagues at Stanford University in 2024 revealed how fungi helped early rice cultivators to brew some of the world’s first beers. As the weather became wetter and warmer, rice naturally attracted a red-coloured mould called Monascus, which secretes enzymes that break down starch into sugar, ready to be converted to alcohol by yeasts in the air. Liu’s team found evidence of this two-stage fungal fermentation process in residues adhering to 10,000-year-old pottery, representing the earliest rice beer in east Asia.

“ Liu and her team’s findings rewrote the history books “

The researchers used highly refined microscopy techniques to identify fragmentary pieces of Monascus on the ancient vessels, as well as granules of starch that showed clear signs of having undergone enzymatic attack. “They were prevented from decomposing by falling into cracks and pores on the surface of the pottery, but it takes a certain skill to use microscopes to identify them,” says Liu. “So this field is still very small, and only our team currently has the necessary expertise to identify these moulds effectively.”

Textual records describing this fungal fermentation process, known as red qu, only go back a couple of millennia, and so Liu and her team’s findings rewrote the history books. “Most scholars believed that red qu-based alcohol was developed about 2000 years ago, so when we discovered that the earliest beers date back to 10,000 years ago, it was a surprise,” says Liu.

These findings indicate that alcohol production began just as rice cultivation took off and people started settling down in farming villages for the first time. In turn, this hints at a central role for booze in helping these early communities thrive. Previously, historians recognised the importance of beer in lubricating political and religious gatherings in major civilisations, including the Egyptian and Inca empires. This new discovery, however, provides the first indication that alcohol was adopted for community-building purposes thousands of years before these powers rose.

Some of the oldest traces of qu were found on pottery vessels retrieved from burial pits, suggesting that beer was connected to mortuary practices. “This tradition probably helped to form a cultural identity and enforce lineage,” says Liu, indicating that these fungal beers played a role in cementing social, political and religious frameworks.

Traditionally, archaeology has been inclined to focus on megalithic monuments’ role in creating group identity, perhaps because they are exactly what fungi are not: durable, conspicuous and willing to give up their stories. Now, a small but growing group of researchers are beginning to tell a quieter tale of human evolution, in which mushrooms helped our ancestors survive the last glacial period and navigate the beginnings of civilisation. “Just as Stonehenge helped Neolithic communities to identify with their land or territory, qu-based alcohol enhanced the rituals that bound people to a particular place or group,” says Liu.

The more we learn, the more we understand that the absence of fungi from the archaeological record says less about their importance to prehistoric humans than it does about our own methodological blind spots.

In other words, the hidden kingdom was always there. We are only just learning how to see it.
articles.reasoning: The article reveals significant archaeological and scientific discoveries about the pivotal role of mushrooms in human history, including survival, medicine, social cohesion, and early fermentation. These findings broaden our understanding of human evolution and ancient cultures, highlighting fungi's broad and meaningful impact on civilization development. The story is focused, detailed, and offers inspiring insights into human ingenuity and the natural world.
articles.personality_title: Ancient mushrooms played key roles in human survival and early societies
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – For thousands of years, mushrooms have been part of human life, but until recently, archaeologists had little proof of how important fungi were to our ancestors. Mushrooms rarely survive in the ground, so their role in history has been hidden.

**What happened** – New scientific methods have allowed researchers to find mushroom remains and fungal DNA in ancient sites and human teeth. For example, 11,000-year-old fire-starting mushrooms were found in England, and evidence shows Neanderthals ate mushrooms and may have used penicillin-like fungi as medicine. The famous Ötzi the Iceman carried fungi possibly used as fishing floats and fire starters. Researchers also discovered that early farmers in Asia used fungi to brew some of the world’s first rice beers, linking mushrooms to social and religious life.

**Impact** – These discoveries change how we see ancient humans. Mushrooms helped with fire, food, medicine, and building communities long before written history. They were part of survival tools for hunter-gatherers and helped early farmers create shared traditions through beer. This shows fungi were more important in human history than previously thought, influencing health, culture, and technology.

**What's next step** – Scientists will continue studying fungal remains and DNA to learn more about ancient diets, medicines, and technologies. New tools might reveal even earlier uses of mushrooms and how they shaped human development. Understanding fungi’s role could change archaeology and give fresh ideas about ancient human life.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Recent discoveries prove mushrooms were vital to early humans for fire, food, medicine, and community, reshaping our understanding of ancient history.
articles.personality_title_fr: Les champignons anciens ont joué un rôle clé dans la survie et les premières sociétés humaines
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Depuis des milliers d’années, les champignons font partie de la vie humaine, mais jusqu’à récemment, les archéologues manquaient de preuves sur leur importance pour nos ancêtres. Les champignons se conservent rarement, ce qui a caché leur rôle dans l’histoire.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – De nouvelles méthodes scientifiques ont permis aux chercheurs de retrouver des champignons anciens et de l’ADN fongique dans des sites archéologiques et des dents humaines. Par exemple, en Angleterre, on a découvert des champignons vieux de 11 000 ans utilisés pour allumer le feu. Des preuves montrent que les Néandertaliens mangeaient des champignons et utilisaient peut-être des champignons semblables à la pénicilline comme médicament. L’homme des glaces Ötzi portait des champignons qui servaient probablement de flotteurs de pêche et d’allume-feu. Des chercheurs ont aussi découvert que les premiers agriculteurs d’Asie utilisaient des champignons pour fabriquer l’une des premières bières de riz, reliant les champignons à la vie sociale et religieuse.

**Impact** – Ces découvertes changent notre regard sur les premiers humains. Les champignons ont aidé au feu, à la nourriture, à la médecine et à la construction des communautés bien avant l’histoire écrite. Ils faisaient partie des outils de survie des chasseurs-cueilleurs et ont aidé les premiers agriculteurs à créer des traditions communes grâce à la bière. Cela montre que les champignons ont eu un rôle plus important dans l’histoire humaine que ce que l’on pensait, influençant la santé, la culture et la technologie.

**Prochaine étape** – Les scientifiques continueront d’étudier les restes fongiques et l’ADN pour mieux comprendre les régimes alimentaires anciens, les médicaments et les technologies. De nouveaux outils pourraient révéler des usages encore plus anciens des champignons et leur influence sur le développement humain. Mieux comprendre le rôle des champignons pourrait transformer l’archéologie et offrir de nouvelles idées sur la vie ancienne.

**Conclusion en une phrase** – Les découvertes récentes prouvent que les champignons étaient essentiels aux premiers humains pour le feu, la nourriture, la médecine et la vie en communauté, modifiant notre compréhension de l’histoire ancienne.
articles.personality_title_es: Los antiguos hongos jugaron un papel clave en la supervivencia y las primeras sociedades humanas
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Durante miles de años, los hongos han formado parte de la vida humana, pero hasta hace poco, los arqueólogos tenían poca evidencia de su importancia para nuestros antepasados. Los hongos rara vez se conservan, por lo que su papel en la historia ha permanecido oculto.

**Qué pasó** – Nuevos métodos científicos permitieron a los investigadores encontrar restos de hongos antiguos y ADN fúngico en sitios arqueológicos y dientes humanos. Por ejemplo, en Inglaterra se hallaron hongos de 11,000 años usados para encender fuego. Hay pruebas de que los neandertales comían hongos y quizá usaban hongos similares a la penicilina como medicina. El famoso hombre de hielo Ötzi llevaba hongos que probablemente usaba como flotadores de pesca y para hacer fuego. También se descubrió que los primeros agricultores en Asia usaban hongos para preparar una de las primeras cervezas de arroz, relacionando los hongos con la vida social y religiosa.

**Impacto** – Estos hallazgos cambian cómo vemos a los humanos antiguos. Los hongos ayudaron con el fuego, la comida, la medicina y la formación de comunidades mucho antes de la historia escrita. Formaron parte de las herramientas de supervivencia de los cazadores-recolectores y ayudaron a los primeros agricultores a crear tradiciones comunes mediante la cerveza. Esto muestra que los hongos fueron más importantes en la historia humana de lo que se pensaba, influyendo en la salud, cultura y tecnología.

**Próximo paso** – Los científicos seguirán estudiando restos de hongos y ADN para aprender más sobre dietas, medicinas y tecnologías antiguas. Nuevas herramientas podrían revelar usos aún más antiguos de los hongos y cómo moldearon el desarrollo humano. Comprender mejor el papel de los hongos podría cambiar la arqueología y aportar nuevas ideas sobre la vida antigua.

**Conclusión en una frase** – Descubrimientos recientes prueban que los hongos fueron vitales para los primeros humanos en el fuego, la comida, la medicina y la comunidad, cambiando nuestra comprensión de la historia antigua.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting of ancient mushrooms—tinder fungi with charred edges and delicate spores—arranged alongside symbolic representations of early human survival tools like birch bark rolls and a glowing ember, set against a softly lit prehistoric peat bog landscape, using natural earthy tones and simple colors to evoke the hidden, vital role of fungi in shaping human history.

articles.title: Keep calm and be transparent: advice from scientists who retracted their papers
articles.summary: Nature, Published online: 10 March 2026; doi:10.1038/d41586-026-00763-xRetractions correct the scientific record, but they have stigma attached to them. Some in the research community want that to change.
articles.content:
Some researchers who retract their papers do so after others in the scientific community raise red flags to them about their work.Credit: EyeEm/Getty

Early last month, evolutionary biologist Nicole King and postdoctoral fellow Jacob Steenwyk retracted their paper in the journal Science1. King was shaken when she realized that the paper — which attempted to use a new data-analysis approach to work out which animal lineages were the first to emerge on Earth — had serious technical errors. But the decision was still clear: “If you know you made a mistake, you’ve got to reverse it,” she says.

Retractions caused by honest mistakes are extremely stressful, say researchers

Retractions, which have long been associated with misconduct or poor scientific practice, can carry a lot of stigma. As of a decade ago, only about 22% of retractions resulted from authors self-reporting errors2, rather than other scientists raising concerns to journals. Authors whose papers are retracted often stop publishing, especially if the retractions are well publicized3.

So King, a researcher at the University of California, Berkeley, was pleasantly surprised to see some scientists celebrating her decision online. “One reason for loving science is how graciously we sometimes deal with errors,” said a bioinformatics researcher on the social-media platform Bluesky.

Research has shown that when authors self-retract because of honest mistakes, their earlier work continues to be cited2. These data, along with anecdotes such as King’s story, suggest that attitudes about retractions might eventually shift. Nature reached out to scientists who have openly retracted their studies, and asked about their experiences and lessons learnt.

“I completely understand why people are scared about it,” but correcting the scientific literature is important, says Ivan Oransky, co-founder of media organization Retraction Watch, which maintains the world’s most comprehensive database of scientific retractions.

Oransky hopes to incentivize researchers with the Ctrl-Z Award — a reference to the ‘undo’ command on a keyboard — launched this week by the Center for Scientific Integrity, Retraction Watch’s parent organization in New York City. Each year, the prize, worth US$2,500, will go to early-career and senior researchers “who discover substantial errors in their published work and take meaningful steps to correct the scientific record”, despite the professional risks.

Advancing science

Benjamin de Haas, now a neuroscientist at Justus Liebig University Giessen in Germany, navigated a retraction early in his career by reminding himself that mistakes happen, and fixing them is an important part of science.

These universities have the most retracted scientific articles

Susanne Stoll, then a PhD student at University College London (UCL), was the first to let Haas know that there might be a problem with a paper he had worked on. She joined the laboratory of one of Haas’s co-authors and was examining some of Haas’s old work. The research found that, when people concentrate on a detailed object, the brain’s vision centre blurs out the object’s surroundings — leading to a kind of ‘tunnel vision’4.

Stoll suspected that the data analysis was flawed and led to an incorrect conclusion. So, along with her supervisor, she e-mailed Haas.

When Haas saw the e-mail, he dug up his old data and, after some frantic analysis, realized that Stoll was right. “That was a real ‘oh shit’ moment,” Haas says.

Even so, he didn’t hesitate to contact the journal Current Biology and ask for a retraction. Although he had just launched his own lab and was worried about his career, Haas wanted to be as transparent as possible, to make it clear that there was no misconduct.

Now, looking back on that time six years ago, he wishes he could tell himself to remain calm: “You’ll end up with tenure anyway.”

Not pointing fingers

What helped Haas was the reaction of his former PhD adviser, Geraint Rees, now a vice-provost at UCL. When it became clear that the paper needed to be retracted, Rees offered Haas support, rather than expressing blame or disappointment, Haas says. Working together on the retraction made it less intimidating.

The way in which Stoll initially approached him was also essential, Haas says. She was kind and respectful, and they worked together to understand the issue. “We had a common goal,” he adds.
articles.reasoning: The article highlights a positive cultural shift in the scientific community towards transparency and integrity by encouraging researchers to self-retract papers with honest errors. This fosters trust in science, reduces stigma around mistakes, and promotes the correction of the scientific record, which benefits society broadly by improving the reliability of scientific knowledge.
articles.personality_title: Scientists encourage honesty by openly correcting their research mistakes
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – In science, sometimes researchers find mistakes in their own published studies. Fixing these errors by retracting papers has often been seen as a bad thing, linked to dishonesty or poor work. But attitudes are starting to change.

**What happened** – Recently, evolutionary biologist Nicole King and her colleague Jacob Steenwyk retracted their paper after discovering serious technical errors. Other scientists praised their honesty online. Another researcher, Benjamin de Haas, also retracted a paper after a student pointed out a data problem. Despite worries about their careers, they chose to be transparent and correct the record.

**Impact** – These examples show that admitting honest errors is becoming more accepted in science. When researchers self-correct, their work can still be trusted and cited. New awards, like the Ctrl-Z Award, encourage scientists to fix mistakes without fear. Supportive reactions from colleagues also help reduce the stigma around retractions.

**What's next step** – The scientific community hopes more researchers will feel confident to report their own mistakes. This will improve the accuracy of scientific knowledge and build trust. Organizations plan to continue promoting transparency and recognizing those who correct the record.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Scientists are increasingly embracing honesty by correcting their own research errors, helping science become more reliable and open.
articles.personality_title_fr: Des scientifiques encouragent l'honnêteté en corrigeant ouvertement leurs erreurs de recherche
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – En science, il arrive que des chercheurs découvrent des erreurs dans leurs propres études publiées. Corriger ces erreurs en retirant des articles était souvent mal vu, associé à un manque d’honnêteté ou à un travail médiocre. Mais les mentalités commencent à évoluer.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Récemment, la biologiste évolutive Nicole King et son collègue Jacob Steenwyk ont retiré leur article après avoir découvert de graves erreurs techniques. D’autres scientifiques ont salué leur honnêteté en ligne. Un autre chercheur, Benjamin de Haas, a aussi retiré un article après qu’une étudiante ait pointé un problème dans les données. Malgré leurs craintes pour leur carrière, ils ont choisi d’être transparents et de corriger le dossier.

**Impact** – Ces exemples montrent que reconnaître des erreurs honnêtes est de plus en plus accepté en science. Quand les chercheurs se corrigent eux-mêmes, leur travail peut toujours être fiable et cité. De nouveaux prix, comme le prix Ctrl-Z, encouragent les scientifiques à corriger leurs erreurs sans crainte. Le soutien des collègues aide aussi à réduire la stigmatisation autour des retraits.

**Prochaine étape** – La communauté scientifique espère que davantage de chercheurs se sentiront confiants pour signaler leurs propres erreurs. Cela améliorera la précision des connaissances scientifiques et renforcera la confiance. Les organisations continueront à promouvoir la transparence et à reconnaître ceux qui corrigent le dossier.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Les scientifiques adoptent de plus en plus l’honnêteté en corrigeant leurs propres erreurs de recherche, rendant la science plus fiable et ouverte.
articles.personality_title_es: Científicos fomentan la honestidad corrigiendo abiertamente sus errores de investigación
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – En la ciencia, a veces los investigadores encuentran errores en sus propios estudios publicados. Corregir estos errores retirando artículos se veía como algo negativo, asociado a deshonestidad o mala práctica. Pero las actitudes están cambiando.

**Qué pasó** – Recientemente, la bióloga evolutiva Nicole King y su colega Jacob Steenwyk retiraron su artículo tras descubrir errores técnicos graves. Otros científicos elogiaron su honestidad en línea. Otro investigador, Benjamin de Haas, también retiró un artículo después de que una estudiante señalara un problema en los datos. A pesar de preocuparse por sus carreras, eligieron ser transparentes y corregir el registro.

**Impacto** – Estos ejemplos muestran que admitir errores honestos es cada vez más aceptado en la ciencia. Cuando los investigadores corrigen sus propios errores, su trabajo sigue siendo confiable y citado. Nuevos premios, como el Premio Ctrl-Z, animan a los científicos a corregir errores sin miedo. El apoyo de colegas también ayuda a reducir el estigma de las retractaciones.

**Próximo paso** – La comunidad científica espera que más investigadores se sientan seguros para reportar sus errores. Esto mejorará la precisión del conocimiento científico y fortalecerá la confianza. Las organizaciones seguirán promoviendo la transparencia y reconociendo a quienes corrigen el registro.

**Conclusión en una frase** – Los científicos están adoptando cada vez más la honestidad al corregir sus propios errores de investigación, ayudando a que la ciencia sea más confiable y abierta.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting of a vintage typewriter with a sheet of paper showing a gently glowing “undo” symbol emerging from it, surrounded by softly illuminated, interconnected scientific tools like microscopes and data charts, set against a calm background of a peaceful library filled with open books and glowing orbs symbolizing transparency and collaboration.

articles.title: Wildlife to replace historical characters on banknotes
articles.summary: Nature was the most popular theme among the public, with the new notes designed to deter counterfeiting.
articles.content: Perera said: "The wildlife of the UK is not separate from our culture. It sits in our football crests, our folklore, our coastlines and our childhoods. Giving it space on something as symbolic as our currency feels both overdue and significant."
articles.reasoning: Replacing historical characters with wildlife on banknotes promotes cultural appreciation of nature, reflecting a meaningful societal shift that connects people with their environment through a widely used medium. The change has broad cultural significance and public support, making it an inspiring positive development.
articles.personality_title: UK banknotes to feature wildlife instead of historical figures
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Banknotes usually show famous people from history. These images represent important parts of a country’s culture and history.

**What happened** – The UK has decided to replace the pictures of historical characters on its banknotes with images of wildlife. This change is based on public opinion, which showed that nature was the most popular theme. The new designs also include features to stop people from making fake banknotes.

**Impact** – Using wildlife on money connects people with the natural world around them in a new way. The UK’s wildlife is part of many aspects of life, like sports team logos and stories. Putting animals on banknotes helps remind people of this connection and shows that nature is an important part of culture.

**What's next step** – The new banknotes will be released to the public in the future. This change might encourage other countries to think about how they represent their culture on money. It could also inspire people to learn more about wildlife and protect it.

**One-sentence takeaway** – The UK’s new banknotes will celebrate wildlife, linking money with nature and culture in a fresh and meaningful way.
articles.personality_title_fr: Les billets britanniques mettront en vedette la faune au lieu des personnages historiques
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Les billets de banque montrent généralement des personnes célèbres de l’histoire. Ces images représentent des parties importantes de la culture et de l’histoire d’un pays.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Le Royaume-Uni a décidé de remplacer les images des personnages historiques sur ses billets par des images de la faune. Ce choix vient d’un sondage où la nature était le thème préféré du public. Les nouveaux billets auront aussi des éléments pour empêcher la contrefaçon.

**Impact** – Utiliser la faune sur la monnaie rapproche les gens du monde naturel d’une nouvelle façon. La faune britannique fait partie de nombreux aspects de la vie, comme les logos d’équipes sportives et les contes populaires. Mettre des animaux sur les billets rappelle cette connexion et montre que la nature est une part importante de la culture.

**Prochaine étape** – Les nouveaux billets seront bientôt mis en circulation. Ce changement pourrait encourager d’autres pays à réfléchir à la manière dont ils représentent leur culture sur leur monnaie. Cela pourrait aussi inspirer les gens à en apprendre plus sur la faune et à la protéger.

**Une phrase clé** – Les nouveaux billets britanniques célébreront la faune, reliant la monnaie à la nature et à la culture de manière nouvelle et significative.
articles.personality_title_es: Los billetes del Reino Unido mostrarán fauna en lugar de personajes históricos
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Los billetes suelen mostrar personas famosas de la historia. Estas imágenes representan partes importantes de la cultura e historia de un país.

**Qué pasó** – El Reino Unido decidió reemplazar las imágenes de personajes históricos en sus billetes por imágenes de animales salvajes. Esta decisión se basó en la opinión pública, que eligió la naturaleza como el tema favorito. Los nuevos diseños también incluyen elementos para evitar la falsificación.

**Impacto** – Usar animales en el dinero conecta a las personas con la naturaleza de una forma nueva. La fauna del Reino Unido está presente en muchas partes de la vida, como en los escudos de equipos deportivos y en leyendas. Poner animales en los billetes ayuda a recordar esta conexión y muestra que la naturaleza es una parte importante de la cultura.

**Próximo paso** – Los nuevos billetes se lanzarán al público pronto. Este cambio podría motivar a otros países a pensar en cómo representan su cultura en su dinero. También podría inspirar a las personas a aprender más sobre la fauna y protegerla.

**Una frase para recordar** – Los nuevos billetes del Reino Unido celebrarán la fauna, uniendo el dinero con la naturaleza y la cultura de una manera nueva y significativa.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm painting of intricately designed UK banknotes featuring native British wildlife like a red fox, a barn owl, and a puffin gracefully integrated into ornate patterns reminiscent of football crests and coastal landscapes, using soft natural tones of greens, browns, and muted blues.
